responses.rawOutput: {"final_answer": ["No, the sprinklers do not run this cycle — the tank is at 15%, which is 20% or below, so the pump diverts to the refill valve.", "Yes, the refill valve activates because the tank level (15%) is at or below 20%.", "The timer activates the pump regardless of rain. Pump draws from tank. Tank is at 15%, which is not above 20% — it is 20% or below. Rule: at/below 20%, pump activates refill valve instead and sends no water to sprinklers. Therefore sprinklers do NOT run.", "Q2: Same condition — tank 15% ≤ 20% — means the pump activates the refill valve. So the refill valve DOES activate.", "Q3: Removing the rain sensor means the timer always fires the pump at 6 AM. Rain status is now irrelevant, so the fact that it rained doesn't stop the pump. Tank is still 15% ≤ 20%, so the pump again diverts to the refill valve and no water reaches the sprinklers. final_answer: No, the sprinklers do not run this cycle — the tank is at 15%, which is 20% or below, so the pump diverts to the refill valve.
Yes, the refill valve activates because the tank level (15%) is at or below 20%.

The timer activates the pump regardless of rain. Since the tank is still at 15% (≤20%), the pump activates the refill valve and no water goes to the sprinklers — the same outcome as questions 1 and 2 (refill valve on, sprinklers off).
working: Q1: No rain in last 24h, so the timer activates the pump at 6 AM. Pump draws from tank. Tank is at 15%, which is not above 20% — it is 20% or below. Rule: at/below 20%, pump activates refill valve instead and sends no water to sprinklers. Therefore sprinklers do NOT run.
Q2: Same condition — tank 15% ≤ 20% — means the pump activates the refill valve. So the refill valve DOES activate.

Q3: Removing the rain sensor means the timer always fires the pump at 6 AM. Rain status is now irrelevant, so the fact that it rained doesn't stop the pump. Tank is still 15% ≤ 20%, so the pump again diverts to the refill valve and no water reaches the sprinklers. Outcome: refill valve activates, sprinklers stay off — identical to the current scenario because the tank level, not the rain, drives the sprinkler-vs-refill decision.

parsedOutput.final_answer: Q1: N4 activates at T=2. Q2: N5 first activates at T=1. Q3: N3, N4, and N5 would never activate. Q4: N4 would activate at T=1.
working: T=0: N1 sends to N2 and N3 simultaneously; both receive at T=0.
N2 forwards to N4 with 2-step delay → message reaches N4 at T=2.
N3 forwards to N4 and N5 with 1-step delay → messages reach N4 and N5 at T=1.
Q2: N5 activates immediately on receiving N3's message at T=1, so N5 first activates at T=1.
Q1: N4 needs both N2 (arrives T=2) and N3 (arrives T=1); the second/last arrival is at T=2, so N4 activates at T=2. (N4 also then sends to N5 at T=2, but that's N5's second activation.)

Q3: Remove N1→N3. N3 never receives input → never activates. N4 requires messages from BOTH N2 and N3; N3's message never comes → N4 never activates. N5's only inputs are from N3 and from N4 (when N4 activates); both are now dead → N5 never activates. N2 still activates (from N1). So N3, N4, N5 never activate.
Q4: N2 delay = 0. N2 receives at T=0, forwards instantly → N4 gets N2's message at T=0. N3's message still reaches N4 at T=1. Second/last arrival is T=1, so N4 activates at T=1.
